medications.indication_rationale: A potent topical corticosteroid suppresses the acute eczematous inflammation of the allergic contact dermatitis and breaks the itch cycle — the source note's prescribed treatment. A potent steroid IS appropriate here because the process is inflammatory/allergic, not fungal or infective. It is prescription-only: over telehealth the AI Doctor recognises the indication and arranges prompt review to obtain it rather than issuing a dose autonomously.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the affected area once daily (per source note); avoid applying to frankly infected skin, and reassess if secondary infection develops
contraindications_in_this_case: Confirm no established secondary bacterial infection before/while starting a potent topical steroid
Requires a prescriber — do not issue autonomously over chat; arrange prompt review

medications.indication_rationale: A daily non-sedating oral antihistamine offers symptomatic itch control per the source note and is a reasonable, low-risk adjunct; a sedating antihistamine at night to aid sleep is an acceptable alternative. It does not treat the underlying dermatitis.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally once daily (dose per source note)

medications.drug_name: Over-the-counter antiseptic cream (e.g. Savlon) currently being applied
medications.indication_rationale: The OTC antiseptic cream the patient is applying is acting as an additional irritant and has exacerbated the dermatitis — the correct action is to STOP it. Continuing or recommending it (or a topical antihistamine/local-anaesthetic 'itch' cream, also common sensitisers) is an error of commission that perpetuates the reaction.

No fever, no spreading induration, no purulence and no lymphadenopathy — the clear serous weeping and light crust of an excoriated contact dermatitis is not infection. Antibiotics are over-treatment here and should be reserved strictly for documented secondary bacterial infection.

The geometric buckle-conforming morphology and intense weeping point to a contact dermatitis, not tinea; empiric antifungal treatment would be misdirected. Reserve only if the lesion fails to respond and a dermatophyte infection is subsequently suspected/confirmed.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop applying the over-the-counter antiseptic cream immediately, and use cool damp compresses on the itchy patch instead of scratching. Do not scratch or pick at the crust — broken skin risks infection.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The antiseptic cream is aggravating the dermatitis, and breaking the itch-scratch cycle preserves the skin barrier and reduces the risk of secondary infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Strictly avoid letting the metal belt buckle (or other metal) touch the skin — stop wearing the offending belt, or tuck the shirt in to place a fabric barrier between the metal and the skin, and consider a nickel-free or coated buckle.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removing the nickel/metal allergen entirely removes the trigger and allows the skin to heal; continued contact perpetuates the reaction.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the prescribed potent topical steroid as a thin layer once daily exactly as directed until the reaction settles, and take the antihistamine for the itch as needed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Consistent once-daily potent topical steroid rapidly shuts down the inflammation and stops the weeping; over-application or under-use both undermine healing.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like an allergic skin reaction to the metal (most often nickel) in your new belt buckle — your skin has reacted where the buckle presses against it. It's a common allergic contact reaction, not an emergency; avoiding the metal removes the trigger and the steroid ointment calms the inflammation.
Stop using the over-the-counter antiseptic cream — antiseptic and 'itch' creams can themselves irritate the skin and are making this worse.
Try not to scratch, and use cool damp compresses for the itch instead. Broken, weeping skin can occasionally get infected, so keep the area clean and watch it.
Once you stop the metal contact and start the steroid ointment, the intense itching and weeping usually settle within a day or two and the patch dries and heals over the following week.

Get same-day review if the redness starts spreading beyond the patch, the clear fluid turns into thick yellow or foul-smelling pus, or you develop a fever or feel unwell — these can mean the skin has become infected.

follow_up_plan.interval: prompt review (in person or telehealth) within 24-48 hours to confirm the diagnosis, inspect the weeping skin and obtain/commence the potent topical corticosteroid, then routine GP review at about 7 days to ensure resolution and discuss long-term allergen (metal) avoidance
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: response of the dermatitis to the topical corticosteroid, skin integrity and any secondary infection of the excoriated plaque, and confirmation that the metal allergen is being avoided
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading redness/heat beyond the patch, thick foul pus, fever, or feeling systemically unwell — seek same-day/urgent care

must_include_items: Recognise a common nickel/metal allergic contact dermatitis (not an emergency) from the geometric belt-buckle correspondence, itch predominance and time-course
Advise STOPPING the aggravating over-the-counter antiseptic cream, and strict avoidance of the metal buckle (remove the belt or use a fabric/nickel-free barrier)
Instruct patient-performable measures: cool compresses instead of scratching and do NOT scratch/pick the crust
Advise a potent topical corticosteroid and an oral antihistamine for the itch, and recognise the topical steroid is prescription-only
Screen for and safety-net secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis with explicit T3-T4 triggers, and arrange PROMPT review to obtain the steroid and lay eyes on the skin
acceptable_alternatives: A sedating oral antihistamine at night to aid sleep is an acceptable alternative to a non-sedating one
Deferring the exact topical-steroid choice/regimen to the reviewing prescriber rather than specifying it sight-unseen is acceptable
Same-day (T3) in-person review is an acceptable, slightly more cautious disposition given the weeping/excoriation and is not scored as an error
A short course of a bland emollient/barrier and simple non-antiseptic wound care while awaiting review is acceptable
errors_of_omission: Failing to advise stopping the aggravating antiseptic cream
Failing to advise strict avoidance of the metal buckle allergen
Failing to screen for/safety-net secondary bacterial infection of the excoriated plaque
Failing to arrange any review in a weeping, excoriated dermatitis that needs a prescription-only potent topical steroid (pure self-care only)
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative red-flag screen straight to 000/ED (over-triage)
Continuing/recommending the OTC antiseptic cream, or a topical antihistamine/local-anaesthetic 'itch' cream (irritants/sensitisers that worsen ACD)
Prescribing empiric antibiotics with no signs of secondary infection (treating serous weeping as pus)
Prescribing a topical antifungal on the assumption of ringworm, missing the contact-dermatitis geometry
Dismissing the presentation as trivial and giving no treatment, allergen advice, follow-up or safety-net
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) recognising the metal allergic contact dermatitis and advising stop-the-antiseptic-cream + metal avoidance + cool compresses/do-not-scratch + a potent topical corticosteroid, (b) an explicit safety-net for secondary bacterial infection / cellulitis (T3, escalating to T4 if systemically unwell/spreading), and (c) arranging prompt review to obtain the prescription-only steroid. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED, an empiric-antibiotic or antifungal answer, or continuing the irritant antiseptic cream is a scored error regardless of other content; so is giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net at all.
